body: Harlan and Wolff and neighbouring Workman, Clark were industrial complexes capable of building engines and many other ship components rather than merely assembling purchased parts. Each could still buy specialist items when useful, but substantial production remained inside the yards. On one occasion the firms were delayed on different engine types needed for separate ships. By exchanging an engine each yard had ahead of schedule, both could meet their delivery dates and recover the lagging work later. The episode reveals how proximity, broad skills, and cooperation within a dense local industrial ecosystem could solve production bottlenecks.

evidence.note: The transcript describes integrated production and the engine exchange between neighbouring yards.

title: Rosyth and the Grand Fleet's maintenance problem
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Rosyth exposes the risk of basing a major fleet where dock capacity lags operational demand.
body: The video contrasts the well-supported southern dockyards with the facilities available to the Grand Fleet in the north. Portsmouth and Devonport possessed multiple docks and basins, but Rosyth initially offered only a lock dock and a single dry dock for a vast concentration of warships. The main basin and further works were still under construction and scheduled for completion in 1916. That timing left the base badly prepared when the First World War began in 1914. Rosyth shows the risk of choosing an operationally useful fleet location before its maintenance infrastructure has caught up.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses Rosyth's limited dock space and delayed construction.

title: Repair capacity as wartime force generation
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Repairing damaged ships reduced the replacement burden and helped the Royal Navy remain effective in a long war.
body: Dockyards support naval expansion by returning damaged ships to service instead of forcing new construction to replace every serious casualty. Dr. Clarke argues that British warship survival improved markedly after 1942 because damaged destroyers and larger vessels could usually be repaired. Britain and the United States had begun expanding yards before entering their periods of maximum wartime production, demonstrating that industrial mobilization took years rather than a sudden turn of a tap. Germany, Italy, and Japan possessed less repair capacity and faced sharper choices between restoring damaged ships and building desperately needed new ones. The result was force generation through maintenance: repaired hulls allowed new construction to increase fleet size instead of merely covering losses.

evidence.note: The transcript connects repair capacity, replacement demand, and World War II fleet endurance.

title: Hong Kong as an imperial repair base
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Hong Kong's naval yard demonstrates how workshops, stores, basins, and a dry dock supported a fleet far from Britain.
body: Hong Kong's 1909 naval yard was a dedicated Royal Navy facility rather than one of the nearby commercial yards at Kowloon or Taikoo. It had its own basin, dry dock, workshops, boat basin, War Department space, and Admiralty stores. That concentration allowed the navy to repair and supply ships in East Asian waters without sending every vessel back toward Britain. Dr. Clarke distinguishes these facilities to show how several kinds of maritime industry could coexist within one imperial port. Singapore's later rise is framed as the next stage in the same strategic problem of sustaining a fleet in the Far East.

evidence.note: Hong Kong's naval yard, associated facilities, and Far East role are described.

title: Bermuda's floating dock as strategic mobility
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Bermuda shows why a movable dock could be more useful politically and strategically than a permanent basin.
body: Bermuda's position made it critical to the Royal Navy's North America and West Indies station, but the size of its floating dock determined which warships the base could support. The first dock was designed for ironclads of roughly 10,000 tons and could accept a vessel longer than itself if the load was balanced correctly. HMS Warrior and HMS Black Prince towed it across the Atlantic, with HMS Terrible acting as a steering guide astern. A mobile dock avoided some difficulties of Bermuda's porous geology and could be removed if a permanent base became politically troublesome after the American Civil War. The case study joins engineering, diplomacy, and station strategy in one piece of movable infrastructure.

evidence.note: The transcript covers Bermuda's location, floating dock limits, construction, towing, and strategic rationale.

title: How a floating dry dock balances and maintains itself
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: The dock's ability to lift ships depended on careful ballast control, and it could be careened for its own upkeep.
body: The Bermuda dock was designed to be careened, either pulled into shallow water or selectively flooded until one side of its own hull became accessible for cleaning. This let the dock maintain itself despite tropical fouling and corrosion. Its ballast compartments also had to submerge and rise in a controlled sequence while supporting a ship's weight. Salt water acted on both the inside and outside of the dock structure, demanding thick material and regular upkeep. A badly positioned ship or uneven flooding could make the entire dock move, turning a small balance error into a dangerous shift of many thousands of tons.

evidence.note: The transcript explains careening, self-maintenance, balancing, corrosion, and the consequences of movement.

title: Why dry docks become strategic targets
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: A question about mobile docks leads to the logic behind raiding and sabotaging repair infrastructure.
body: The answer treats docks as obvious targets because disabling them can immobilize warships without sinking every hull. German and Italian planners had reason to hunt British floating docks, while plans for attacks such as Pearl Harbor also considered fixed repair facilities. Britain repeatedly bombed, mined, raided, or demolished enemy dock infrastructure in continental Europe. A destroyer packed with explosives could deny a large graving dock to the enemy for far longer than a conventional raid on an individual ship. The exchange makes repair infrastructure part of operational targeting and explains why dock locations appear so prominently in naval intelligence.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses attacks on British, French, Dutch, Belgian, and other dock facilities.

question: Were enemy floating docks and repair facilities priority targets for naval planners?
answerShort: Yes. Destroying or disabling docks restricts an opponent's ability to repair warships, so planners repeatedly treated them as high-value targets.

title: Floating docks for forward fleet support
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: A range of floating-dock sizes let the Royal Navy support everything from small craft to battleships at forward stations.
body: The video surveys floating docks ranging from small lifts for torpedo boats to 55,000- and 60,000-ton facilities capable of supporting battleships. Their deployments followed operational demand: docks went to Reykjavík for the North Atlantic convoy war, Sydney for Pacific support, Bermuda and Singapore for overseas stations, and Scapa Flow for the Home Fleet. These were staffed technical commands rather than empty pontoons. A mixed crew of engineers and naval constructors operated ballast systems and travelling cranes, while welders and other trades arrived from repair ships or shore establishments. Mobile docks therefore worked as part of a larger support package that could move maintenance capacity toward a fleet.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses dock capacities, deployments, crews, and forward repair roles.

title: Can a floating dock service another navy's ship?
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: The final technical answer sets out the practical conditions for using a dock with an unfamiliar warship.
body: A floating dock can service a foreign vessel when its lifting capacity and physical dimensions are adequate for the hull. National ownership does not create a technical barrier by itself. Operators must know the vessel's displacement, weight distribution, pressure points, and centre of mass before arranging the supporting blocks and ballast. Those details prevent the unfamiliar hull from being loaded in the wrong places or upsetting the dock's balance. Repair personnel and suitable equipment must then be available, just as they would be for one of the owning navy's ships.

evidence.note: The answer specifies capacity, pressure points, centre of mass, and repair personnel.

question: Could one navy's floating dry dock service ships from another navy?
answerShort: Yes, if the ship fits within the dock's capacity and operators understand its support points and balance requirements.

title: Palmer's and the depth of Britain's shipbuilding market
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Palmer's warship record and the national yard list reveal how many British firms could build substantial naval vessels.
body: Palmer's had built the battlecruiser HMS Queen Mary, battleships including HMS Russell and HMS Resolution, the cruiser HMS York, destroyers, and monitors before the company failed. Dr. Clarke uses that record to show that even a bankrupt yard could have possessed serious warship expertise and infrastructure. He then surveys builders across England, Scotland, Wales, and Belfast that could produce Flower-class vessels or larger ships. The list excludes the many smaller yards, making it a conservative picture of industrial depth rather than a complete inventory. This section gives useful scale to Britain's ability to distribute naval and merchant work among numerous private firms.

evidence.note: Palmer's major warships and a geographically broad list of British yards capable of building Flower-class ships or larger are discussed.

title: The 1909 dockyard survey confronts growing warships
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Royal Navy maps measured docks, basins, and expansion space because new warships were outgrowing existing facilities.
body: The maps shown in the video come from a 1909 Royal Navy survey of port and dockyard infrastructure. Warships were becoming larger, and the Admiralty needed to know which facilities could receive them and which sites could be enlarged. The survey therefore recorded dry docks, basins, locks, cranes, and proposed extensions rather than merely marking shipyards by name. Nearby coal and iron supplies influenced where shipbuilding developed, while large fitting-out basins provided room to complete newly launched hulls. The episode turns these plans into evidence of institutional preparation for the dreadnought era and its growing infrastructure demands.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies the 1909 survey, connects it to increasing ship size, and discusses coal, iron, fitting-out space, and dock acreage.

title: Matching Neptune-class battleships to usable docks
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: A 1909 compatibility table shows exactly where the Royal Navy's newest battleships could be docked at home and overseas.
body: The survey tested naval infrastructure against the Neptune class, then the Royal Navy's newest battleship type. Portsmouth and Devonport offered several options, while Gibraltar, Malta, Haulbowline, Hong Kong, and Rosyth provided selected overseas or regional berths. Some docks could accept a Neptune only when a movable caisson was placed at its outer stop, adding operational complication. Smaller cruisers fit many more facilities, revealing how rapidly support options narrowed as hull dimensions increased. The table makes global deployability a measurable relationship between a ship's size and a network of specific docks.

evidence.note: The 1909 table identifies docks at Portsmouth, Devonport, Haulbowline, Gibraltar, Malta, Hong Kong, and Rosyth that could take a Neptune-class battleship.

title: Why heavy warships were fitted out in graving docks
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Dock support and staged loading protected a newly launched hull while propellers, superstructure, and heavy turrets were installed.
body: A large warship could be launched in relatively light condition with its machinery and shafts aboard, then moved into a graving dock for heavier fitting-out work. Workers positioned wooden blocks to support the hull, drained the dock, and installed propellers and superstructure with direct access below the waterline. The dock could then be flooded before the heaviest turret structures were loaded so buoyancy helped support the hull. Dr. Clarke warns that badly sequenced weight could distort a ship and alarm its constructors. The stepped sides and crane access of a modern dock were therefore part of the construction process as well as later maintenance.

evidence.note: Cammell Laird enlargement, light launch condition, block placement, propeller and superstructure fitting, turret weight, and stepped dock access are explained.

title: Haulbowline: enlarging an island for a naval dockyard
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: The Royal Navy doubled Haulbowline Island to create a repair base, large dock, fuel depot, and hospital in Cork Harbour.
body: The Royal Navy established Haulbowline in Cork Harbour during the 1860s as a repair and construction base behind the western approaches. Accommodating the industrial complex required the island to be enlarged to roughly sixty acres, making much of the site artificial ground. Its first major dry dock was about 408 feet long and was extended to roughly 600 feet shortly before the First World War. Plans left room for further growth if larger ships demanded it. A coal and fuel depot and naval hospital made Haulbowline a full support base, illustrating the lengths the Admiralty would take to create strategically placed infrastructure.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Haulbowline's establishment, island enlargement, dock extension, coal and fuel depot, and hospital.

title: Pembroke built ships while Portsmouth sustained the Channel Fleet
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Contrasting dockyard plans show how slipway-heavy construction sites and dock-rich fleet bases served different naval purposes.
body: Pembroke's plan is dominated by numerous building slips and relatively little docking space, marking it primarily as a warship construction yard. Its single dock and smaller haul-out arrangements could handle maintenance, but the layout privileged launching new hulls. Portsmouth presents the opposite emphasis, with deep docks, locks, basins, and multiple repair berths beside the fleet base. Those facilities allowed the Channel Fleet's routine maintenance needs to be met close to its operational station. The comparison shows why the word dockyard can conceal very different balances among construction, fitting-out, and repair work.

evidence.note: Pembroke's slips and maintenance dock are contrasted with Portsmouth's extensive facilities and support for the Channel Fleet.

title: How Britain's largest graving docks compare worldwide
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: A size comparison places the United Kingdom's largest docks beside much longer modern Asian shipbuilding facilities.
body: Dr. Clarke identifies Seaton Port on Teesside as the United Kingdom's longest graving dock at roughly 376 metres. Belfast, Rosyth, Inchgreen, and Cammell Laird provide other large British examples, while proposed Portsmouth work would reach roughly 310 metres. He contrasts them with facilities in Taiwan, China, and South Korea extending about 700 to 950 metres. British docks that ranked among the world's largest when built are therefore much smaller than leading modern construction sites. The comparison explains why retained infrastructure can remain useful while no longer offering the strategic margin it once did.

evidence.note: The answer lists Seaton Port and other major UK docks, then compares them with much longer facilities in Taiwan, China, and South Korea.

question: What is the largest graving dock in the United Kingdom, and how does it compare with the world's largest?
answerShort: Seaton Port is identified as Britain's longest at about 376 metres, while leading modern Asian docks reach roughly 700 to 950 metres.

title: How ships were moved inside dockyard basins
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Tugs, locomotives, and winches moved ships slowly through confined basins where their own propulsion was unsuitable.
body: Some large basins had room for tugboats to position a vessel, but tighter dockyards used shore machinery instead. Small steam locomotives, capstans, or winches could haul a ship along carefully arranged lines from the quay. Operators wanted controlled movement rather than high power because a heavy hull had little room to recover from a mistake. The method also let an incomplete or disabled ship move without relying on its own engines. This short answer supplies the practical link between a dockyard plan and the everyday handling needed to use it.

evidence.note: The answer names tugboats, steam dockyard locomotives, and winches and stresses deliberately slow movement.

question: Were ships moved around dockyard basins by tugboats or under their own power?
answerShort: Both were possible, but confined basins often used tugboats, shore locomotives, or winches to move ships as slowly and safely as possible.

title: Portsmouth's 1495 dry dock survives with HMS M33
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: The oldest surviving early-modern European dry dock remains visible at Portsmouth, where it now holds HMS M33.
body: Dr. Clarke identifies the dock commissioned by Henry VII at Portsmouth in 1495 as the oldest surviving early-modern European dry dock still in use. The structure's endurance links Tudor naval administration with the modern historic dockyard. HMS M33, a First World War monitor, now occupies the dock and makes its original function easy to visualize. Continued reuse also demonstrates how waterfront infrastructure can outlive successive generations of ships when its dimensions suit new purposes. The answer is both a naval-engineering fact and a guide to a surviving physical site.

evidence.note: The transcript dates the Portsmouth dock to Henry VII in 1495 and identifies HMS M33 as its present occupant.

question: What is the oldest surviving dry dock still in use?
answerShort: The Portsmouth dock commissioned by Henry VII in 1495 is identified as the oldest surviving early-modern European example, and it now holds HMS M33.

title: Shipyards could build aircraft, locomotives, guns, and tanks
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: A question about wartime diversification explains why major shipbuilders could shift their machinery and skills into other heavy products.
body: Dr. Clarke describes firms such as Armstrong Whitworth and Vickers as broad heavy-engineering businesses rather than narrowly specialized ship assemblers. Their works produced combinations of aircraft, armor, bridges, cars, cranes, locomotives, guns, tanks, and ships. Existing foundries, machine shops, design skills, and large-scale material handling made diversification technically plausible. Firms followed profitable markets in peacetime and could redirect capacity toward wartime needs. The answer helps explain why the health of a shipbuilding region affected a much wider defense-industrial base.

evidence.note: The answer lists aircraft, armor, bridges, cars, cranes, locomotives, ships, tanks, and other products made by heavy-engineering firms.

question: Could shipyards and dockyard industry build tanks and artillery, or did they diversify only during wartime?
answerShort: Major yards were diversified heavy-engineering firms and routinely pursued other products when markets existed, giving them the capacity to build weapons and vehicles in wartime.

title: Malta's docks supported battleships in the Mediterranean
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Several Malta docks could receive a Neptune-class battleship, while caisson position and crane access affected how useful each berth was.
body: Malta's dockyard offered several ways to receive a Neptune-class battleship in the Mediterranean. Docks Five and Six could be combined for a capital ship, Dock Seven provided another berth, and Dock Four worked only with its caisson at the outer position. That difference made nominal length less important than the exact operating configuration of each dock. Travelling cranes beside the combined docks improved access for removing or installing heavy equipment. Malta consequently served as a genuine fleet repair hub rather than merely a harbour at a strategically convenient island.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies Malta docks able to take Neptune, explains the outer caisson requirement, and notes travelling cranes beside Docks Five and Six.

title: Halifax linked its dry dock and coaling wharf to rail supply
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Halifax's railways, coaling wharf, hospital, and dry dock show how land transport fed an Atlantic naval base.
body: The 1904 Halifax plan combines a substantial dry dock with a naval coaling wharf, anchor wharf, hospital, and extensive railway access. Rail lines carried the heavy bulk materials that a fleet base consumed in quantities impractical for road transport. Coal, armor, machinery, stores, and other loads could move directly between the national transport network and the waterfront. The supporting hospital and specialized wharves broadened the base beyond ship repair alone. Halifax demonstrates how inland logistics and maritime infrastructure had to meet at the same site to sustain Atlantic operations.

evidence.note: The Halifax plan includes a dry dock, railway lines, naval coaling and anchor wharves, and a hospital, with rail explained as essential for bulk supply.

title: Cockatoo Island and Simon's Town anchored southern imperial routes
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Island docks at Sydney and a lasting base near Cape Town extended repair capacity across southern sea routes.
body: At Sydney, Cockatoo Island used the Fitzroy and Sutherland docks together with nearby Mort's Dock and the protected waters around the island. The Royal Navy and colonial authorities adapted limited island space into a concentrated repair and construction complex, much as they had at Haulbowline. Simon's Town near Cape Town occupied another strategic point on imperial sea routes and remained South Africa's principal naval base. Its modern outline still follows much of the layout visible in the 1909 plan, despite later additions. Together the sites show how durable regional dockyards supported fleets far from Britain and influenced later national naval infrastructure.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses Cockatoo Island's docks and protected basin, then Simon's Town's location, 1909 importance, and continuity as a naval base.

title: Esquimalt and Auckland carried dockyard capacity into the Pacific
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Canadian and New Zealand docks extended the repair network across the Pacific and remained useful long after 1909.
body: Esquimalt's Canadian government dry dock gave the Royal Navy and later Canadian forces a repair point on the Pacific coast of North America. Although the 1909 base was modest, its location worried American planners more than British officials emphasized it. Auckland's Calliope Dock supplied a corresponding facility in New Zealand waters. Originally about 160 metres long, Calliope was extended repeatedly in 1936, 1943, and 1996, demonstrating how an old dock could remain relevant through sustained enlargement. These sites made global deployment more credible by giving damaged ships regional alternatives to a voyage back to Britain.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Esquimalt's Canadian government dock and strategic location, then gives Calliope Dock's original size and later extensions.

title: HMS Gorgon became too wide for Norway's docks
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Royal Navy modifications made the former Norwegian monitor too broad for the facilities at its intended operating bases.
body: HMS Gorgon had originally been built for Norway, but Royal Navy modifications increased her beam. The resulting ship no longer fit the Norwegian dry docks in the areas where she was intended to operate. Norway therefore faced a warship it could not maintain within its existing support network. The example makes dock compatibility an acquisition requirement rather than an administrative detail discovered after delivery. A design improvement can reduce practical value when it breaks the relationship between a ship and its bases.

evidence.note: The transcript says Royal Navy modifications made HMS Gorgon too wide for Norwegian docks in her intended operating areas.

title: Japan's fleet lacked enough mobile repair capacity
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: More floating docks could have separated Japanese warship construction from forward repair and eased a critical wartime bottleneck.
body: Dr. Clarke applies the floating-dock lesson to the Imperial Japanese Navy's prewar industrial problem. Japanese fixed yards were needed for construction, yet the fleet also required repair capacity across a vast operating area. Building or acquiring more floating docks could have moved maintenance away from scarce construction berths and closer to forward forces. Older foreign docks might also have supplemented domestic production. Japan's failure to develop enough of this infrastructure becomes an example of disconnected planning between fleet ambition, shipbuilding, and long-term support.

evidence.note: The transcript argues that Japan could have maximized fixed construction yards by building or acquiring more floating dry docks for fleet support.

title: Why the modern Royal Navy lacks a large floating-dock fleet
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: The short answer connects today's absence of many floating docks to the much smaller number of ships they would support.
body: Dr. Clarke gives a direct force-structure explanation for the Royal Navy's loss of this once-extensive capability. A large fleet spread across worldwide stations could justify numerous specialized mobile docks and their crews. The modern navy operates far fewer ships and therefore has less routine demand for such a network. Maintaining idle docks would itself consume money, personnel, and waterfront support. The answer underlines that infrastructure should be assessed against the fleet and deployment pattern it is intended to sustain.

evidence.note: The transcript answers that the Royal Navy no longer has enough ships to justify the docks.

question: Why does the Royal Navy no longer operate many floating dry docks?
answerShort: Its present fleet is too small to justify maintaining the extensive floating-dock network required by the former global navy.

title: What distinguishes a nuclear-capable shipyard
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Nuclear work adds reactor-support facilities, security, and procedures to the ordinary requirements of a naval yard.
body: A nuclear-capable yard still performs conventional hull and machinery work, but it must also support a reactor that cannot simply be treated as inert equipment. Dr. Clarke emphasizes additional procedures, security measures, monitoring, and specialist facilities. Even when a reactor is shut down, residual conditions require continued management rather than abandonment. The yard therefore needs trained personnel and systems dedicated to nuclear safety alongside ordinary shipwright skills. The answer explains why not every large dry dock can automatically maintain a nuclear-powered warship.

evidence.note: The answer identifies procedures, security, reactor-support facilities, and continued reactor management as the distinguishing requirements.

question: What is the difference between a conventional shipyard and a nuclear-capable shipyard?
answerShort: A nuclear yard adds specialized reactor-support facilities, monitoring, security, and procedures to conventional ship-repair capability.

title: Mare Island and the danger of inherited infrastructure
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: A once-leading American yard illustrates how major powers can let valuable facilities age without continued enlargement.
body: Mare Island was regarded as an outstanding purpose-built naval facility when established in the nineteenth century. Its later docks included a roughly 226-metre berth completed in 1910 and another of about 211 metres completed during the Second World War. Dr. Clarke is surprised that the yard did not receive more extensive enlargement afterward. He frames the problem as a major-power tendency to inherit abundant infrastructure and then postpone investment because capacity already appears adequate. The answer complements the British examples by showing that even a rich navy can lose flexibility when docks cease to grow with its ships.

evidence.note: The answer dates Mare Island and its later docks, praises its original design, and criticizes subsequent failure to enlarge the infrastructure.

question: How does Mare Island compare as a naval shipyard?
answerShort: It was world-class when built, but later underinvestment left its dock capacity increasingly dated despite its strong original design.

title: Why Britain avoided a Second World War repair-yard shortage
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Retained docks remained commercially valuable even when their original shipbuilding companies failed, preserving wartime repair capacity.
body: Dr. Clarke argues that the Royal Navy did not suffer a severe general shortage of repair yards during the Second World War because most of the relevant facilities remained open. Shipbuilding companies could fail, but a useful dry dock rarely became valueless. New owners had strong reasons to buy and operate the physical infrastructure even when the original firm disappeared. Retention mattered only when docks were maintained and enlarged enough for contemporary ships; an untouched nineteenth-century berth could become too small. The answer separates the fortunes of corporate names from the survival of strategically valuable waterfront assets.

evidence.note: The answer says Britain retained most wartime repair yards and explains that dry docks were commonly purchased and reused after companies failed.

question: Did the Royal Navy suffer a shortage of repair yards in the Second World War, and could closed docks be reopened?
answerShort: Not generally: most docks remained in use, and valuable repair facilities usually found new owners even when shipbuilding firms failed.

title: Queen Elizabeth-class battleships narrowed the dock network
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: The jump from Neptune to the larger Queen Elizabeth class forced upgrades and sharply reduced the number of overseas docks available.
body: The 1909 survey used the 166-metre Neptune as its leading battleship case, while the later Queen Elizabeth class reached roughly 196 metres overall. That growth removed many docks from the usable network unless they were enlarged or reconfigured. Some British and Mediterranean facilities could accommodate the new ships after work, but overseas choices became much scarcer than for Neptune. The class therefore translated a thirty-metre increase in hull length into a significant logistical constraint. The answer shows why each generation of capital ships triggered another round of dockyard investment.

evidence.note: The answer compares Neptune and Queen Elizabeth lengths and explains that upgrades were required and overseas dock choices declined.

question: Which dry docks could the Queen Elizabeth-class battleships use during the First World War?
answerShort: Far fewer than the Neptune class; several home and Mediterranean docks needed upgrades, and overseas options quickly became limited.
